metadata.working_title: An Atlas of Brain-Bone Sympathetic Neural Circuits Revealed by PRV152 Transneuronal Tracing

abstract_sketch.problem: The sympathetic nervous system (SNS) regulates bone remodeling and mass, but the identity of the central nodes that deliver SNS outflow to bone is unknown in the mouse.
abstract_sketch.gap: Prior neuroanatomical work has mapped limited circuitry for rat femoral marrow, and SNS control of adipose depots is well described, yet a comprehensive central atlas for bone-directed SNS drive in the mouse has been missing.
abstract_sketch.approach: We injected the retrograde, replication-competent, EGFP-expressing pseudorabies virus PRV152 (4.7 x 10^9 pfu/mL; 5 x 150 nL loci) into the metaphysis and periosteum of the right femur of adult male mice (N = 6), then performed serial brain sectioning with anti-EGFP immunofluorescence and manual cell counting every sixth 25 micrometer section across the neuroaxis.
abstract_sketch.key_results: Four of six animals yielded analyzable, bilateral PRV152 labeling spanning 87 nuclei, sub-nuclei and regions across six divisions. The hypothalamus contained the largest number of labeled neurons (1177.25 +/- 62.75), followed by midbrain and pons (1065 +/- 22.39), hindbrain medulla (495.25 +/- 33.49), forebrain (237.5 +/- 15.08), cerebral cortex (104.75 +/- 4.64) and thalamus (65.25 +/- 7.78). Hot-spots included the lateral hypothalamus, paraventricular hypothalamic nucleus and dorsomedial hypothalamus; the periaqueductal gray, lateral periaqueductal gray and pontine reticular nucleus oral part; the raphe pallidus, raphe magnus and gigantocellular reticular nucleus; the medial preoptic nucleus, bed nucleus of the stria terminalis and ventral lateral septum; the primary somatosensory cortex hindlimb region together with M1 and M2 motor cortex; and the periventricular fiber system and precommisural nucleus of the thalamus.
abstract_sketch.impact: This atlas of central SNS outflow to bone establishes anatomical substrates for autonomic regulation of bone metabolism and descending modulation of bone pain, and nominates the raphe magnus to periaqueductal gray axis as a candidate pain-relevant node.

sections.title: Introduction
content_bullets: SNS tone promotes bone loss by suppressing formation and enhancing resorption, as established by Elefteriou, Takeda and colleagues.
Leptin acts anti-osteogenically through ventromedial hypothalamic glucose-responsive neurons that feed peripheral SNS pathways.
Periosteum and marrow are densely innervated by noradrenergic fibers stained by tyrosine hydroxylase, dopamine beta hydroxylase and neuropeptide Y, with VIP-positive fibers in the parenchyma.
Pseudorabies virus Bartha K strain provides a retrograde transneuronal tracer that chains across synapses to map multi-synaptic SNS circuits in a single animal.

Our group has used PRV to trace central SNS outflow to white and brown adipose depots and to PDE5A-containing neurons relevant to bone; a hierarchical circuit for rat femoral marrow was described by Denes and colleagues.
Yet no comprehensive map of the central SNS circuitry innervating the murine femur exists. This study produces one.

sections.title: Methods
content_bullets: Adult male mice (3-4 months old), N=6, single-housed under 12:12 light:dark at 22 +/- 2 C with ad libitum water and chow.

Right femur-tibia joint exposed under isoflurane (2-3% in O2); PRV152 (4.7 x 10^9 pfu/mL) injected as five loci of 150 nL each across metaphysis and periosteum; 60 s syringe dwell; sutures and wound clips; topical nitrofurazone.

Sacrifice 6 days post last injection; transcardial perfusion with 0.9% heparinized saline then 4% paraformaldehyde in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.4); post-fix 3-4 h at 4 C; cryoprotect in 30% sucrose/0.1 M PBS with 0.1% sodium azide.
Sectioning on freezing stage sliding microtome at 25 micrometers; free-floating sections blocked in 10% normal goat serum with 0.4% Triton X-100 in 0.1 M PBS for 30 min.
Primary chicken anti-EGFP (1:1000; Thermo Fisher A10262) for 18 h; secondary AlexaFluor-488 goat anti-chicken (1:700; Jackson Immunoresearch 103-545-155) for 2 h with 2% NGS and 0.4% Triton X-100.
Imaging with 10x and 20x on Observer.Z1 (Zeiss) with AlexaFluor-488 and DAPI filters; Zen and ImageJ for overlay; counts performed every sixth section with manual Photoshop tags.
Negative control: PRV152 placed on bone surface produced no EGFP signal in PVH or RPa. IML labeling at T13-L2 confirmed the expected bone-SNS ganglia-IML-brain route.

subsections.title: Validation of PRV152 transneuronal tracing from bone
content_bullets: Four of six PRV152-injected mice yielded uniform labeling across the neuroaxis and were included; two were excluded for cloudy plaque over-infection.
Unilateral injection produced bilateral brain labeling with no inter-hemispheric dominance, consistent with prior PRV152 tracing of fat depots.
Control: PRV152 applied to the bone surface gave no EGFP in PVH or RPa.
PRV152 injected into periosteum or metaphysis yielded strong EGFP in PVH; IML labeling at T13-L2 confirmed the expected peripheral-to-central route.

subsections.title: Distribution of PRV152-labeled neurons across six brain divisions
content_bullets: Identified 87 PRV152-positive brain nuclei, sub-nuclei and regions across six divisions.

Per-division counts (mean +/- SEM): hypothalamus 1177.25 +/- 62.75; midbrain and pons 1065 +/- 22.39; hindbrain medulla 495.25 +/- 33.49; forebrain 237.5 +/- 15.08; cerebral cortex 104.75 +/- 4.64; thalamus 65.25 +/- 7.78.
Representative photomicrographs in PAG, RPa, LH, MPOM, S1HL and pv confirm labeling across divisions.

subsections.title: Dominant nuclei within each division
content_bullets: Hypothalamus (25 nuclei): LH, PVH, DM carried the largest relative and absolute counts.
Midbrain and pons (18 nuclei): PAG, lateral PAG (LPAG), pontine reticular nucleus oral part (PnO) dominated.
Medulla (23 nuclei): RPa, RMg, gigantocellular reticular nucleus (Gi) were the heaviest hubs.
Forebrain (15 nuclei): MPOM, bed nucleus of the stria terminalis (BST), ventral lateral septal nucleus (LSV).
Cerebral cortex (3 regions): S1HL, M2, M1; S1HL and M2 had the highest counts.
Thalamus (3 sites): periventricular fiber system (pv) and precommisural nucleus (PrC) were dominant.

sections.title: Discussion
content_bullets: First murine femur-based atlas of central SNS outflow; anchors prior adipose-depot and marrow-tracing work in a shared organ-selective framework.
Overlap between femur-projecting and marrow-projecting circuits (PAG, S1HL, MPOM, pv, PVH, lateral hypothalamus, RPa) suggests largely shared, with site-specific, SNS drive.
PAG receives afferents from parabrachial nucleus, RPa and spinal cord and is a major SNS and descending-pain hub; it is a plausible integrator of bone pain and outflow.
PVH and LH as primary hypothalamic outflow nodes; LH, PVH, DM also express leptin receptors, connecting leptin's anti-osteogenic action to this circuit.
RMg-PAG candidate ascending axis for bone pain perception; raphe projections to dorsal horn regulate enkephalin release.
Limitations: male mice only, N=4 analyzable animals, PRV152 time-point fixed at 6 days; these results await extension to females and other mammals.
Implications: tractable targets for pain modulation and for pharmacological control of bone metabolism.
